title: 1.4x higher risk for meningiomas
summary: 1.4x higher risk for meningiomas
detail: This common variant (HapMap allele frequency of 31.3%) in a protein involved in folate (B9) and cobalamin (B12) metabolism and is often reported as "MTRR I22M" (for the Ile to Met substitution at amino acid 22). Mothers homozygous for this variant were found in a few studies to have an increased risk for Down syndrome babies (risk of 0.4%, average risk in population is 0.25%), but other studies (including in Italian, Irish, French, and Indian-Gujarati women) found no increased risk. Notably, age plays a far larger role in the rate of Down syndrome (risk is 4.5% for a mother 45-years-of-age), and, it is unknown how this variant may combine with the effect of age. There are conflicting reports associating this variant with incidence of neural tube defects, possibly when combined with MTHFR A222V.
whyItMatters: SNPedia has a genotype-specific page for this result, which can add consumer-facing context alongside primary evidence sources.
conditions: This common variant (HapMap allele frequency of 31.3%) in a protein involved in folate (B9) and cobalamin (B12) metabolism and is often reported as "MTRR I22M" (for the Ile to Met substitution at amino acid 22). Mothers homozygous for this variant were found in a few studies to have an increased risk for Down syndrome babies (risk of 0.4%, average risk in population is 0.25%), but other studies (including in Italian, Irish, French, and Indian-Gujarati women) found no increased risk. Notably, age plays a far larger role in the rate of Down syndrome (risk is 4.5% for a mother 45-years-of-age), and, it is unknown how this variant may combine with the effect of age. There are conflicting reports associating this variant with incidence of neural tube defects, possibly when combined with MTHFR A222V.

title: Li-Fraumeni syndrome?? (an inherited cancer predisposition - but see note below)
summary: Li-Fraumeni syndrome?? (an inherited cancer predisposition - but see note below)
detail: This genotype is associated with Li Fraumeni syndrome. However, we have noticed a statistically unlikely increase in the number of customers of one DNA testing company who's genotype for this SNP is being incorrectly reported. This is likely to be a technical error - you probably actually carry the completely normal, unmutated genotype for this SNP. You should seek to have a clinically validated test carried out if you have either a significant family history of cancer or if you have any concerns about this. See https://www.reddit.com/r/23andme/comments/2xit8m/how_does_one_interpret_significance/
